text:
High Resolution X-ray Computed Tomography (HRXCT), or micro-CT (µCT), has become a prevalent non-destructive 3D imaging tool for elucidating the internal structure of a wide variety of materials and has become a fundamental tool in the geosciences. Although the Earth science community has adopted the technique as a valuable research tool, the increasing demand for the use of the technique necessitates improvements to push the technique to its physical limits. Therefore, it is crucial that research is being done to address and understand the limitations and challenges of the high resolution X-ray CT technique. This article will provide an in-depth review of the X-ray CT technique, including its theory, benefits and restrictions, as well as a summary of the current uses of X-ray CT in geological research.

One of the main advantages of µCT is its non-destructive nature allowing for 4D in situ observation of the internal structure of samples at sub-micron resolution. In contrast, 3D image analysis can still show a high degree of subjectivity depending on the skill of the operator, and the process of discretization is inherent to any CT measurement, as are specific artifacts related to the method. As computer power and camera technology are progressing at an extraordinary rate, micro-CT is developing from a special-purpose laboratory technique into a common laboratory diagnostic method, and is, therefore, bound to become an important resource in the geosciences in the coming years.
